Recent changes: The "no loyalty for < 2 years" boundary test passed immediately (>=2 guard already in place); no implementation change in Green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Attempt at least one refactoring or document why none is warranted (brief is fine — no code changed in Green).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 19 tests pass. Now let me work through the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase that just completed added no implementation code — the "no loyalty for < 2 years" boundary test passed immediately because the `>= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` guard in `loyaltyDiscount` already handled it.

Naming review of the touched code path:
- `loyaltyDiscount` — clearly reveals intent: returns the loyalty discount amount. Good.
- `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` (= 2) and `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` (= 0.2) — both clearly named. Good.
- The guard `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` reads naturally as "at least the threshold years."

No naming change warranted in the Green-touched code.

## Step 2: Candidate refactoring — the obvious smell

There is a clear, documented smell elsewhere in the file: `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` (lines 86-97). Its own comment admits it is a TEMPORARY hack that misplaces the policy-wide first-insurance surcharge onto individual cursed items. However, that smell is owned by an upcoming test — the explicit todo `"should add 10% first-insurance surcharge on the policy base premium"` (line 249) and `"should apply the curse surcharge only to the cursed item's base premium..."` (line 254). Refactoring it now would require changing behavior, which the Refactor phase must not do (tests must stay green and behavior unchanged). I will leave that for its driving test.

Let me look for a behavior-preserving improvement in the code as it stands.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests pass):** All 19 green. Confirmed.

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** The `loyaltyDiscount` signature is `(customer: Customer, basePremium: number)`. It takes the whole `Customer` only to read one field, while its sibling policy-level helpers operate on plain numbers. There's a mild inconsistency, but passing `Customer` reads clearly ("does this customer qualify"), so no change improves clarity meaningfully.

**Rule 3 (No duplication):** Looking for genuine duplication. None of the surcharge helpers duplicate logic — each computes a distinct rate against a distinct condition.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** Examining `loyaltyDiscount`'s APP mass for a possible simplification.

## APP Mass — `loyaltyDiscount` (current)

```
const loyaltyDiscount = (customer, basePremium) =>
  customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD
    ? basePremium * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE
    : 0;
```
- Bindings: `customer`, `basePremium`, `yearsWithMHPCO`(access), `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`, `basePremium`, `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` ≈ 6 → 6
- Constant: `0` → 1
- Invocations: `>=`, `*` → 2×2 = 4
- Conditional: `?:` → 4
- **Total ≈ 15**

Any rewrite (e.g. `(years >= T ? rate : 0) * base`) keeps the same conditional + multiply structure — no mass reduction, and would lose the explicit `basePremium * RATE` reading. Rule 2 says keep clarity over shaving mass. No change.

## Decision

**Refactoring Evaluation — no code change warranted this cycle:**

- Naming: `loyaltyDiscount`, `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`, `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` already reveal intent precisely. No rename improves them.
- The Green phase added zero implementation — the boundary test passed on the existing `>= 2` guard, so there is no fresh code to clean up.
- The one real smell in the file (`firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly`) is a behavior-bearing hack explicitly reserved for upcoming tests (lines 249, 254). Touching it now would change behavior and break the green-only discipline of Refactor. It must be driven out by its own test, not refactored speculatively.
- Duplication (Rule 3): none found among the modifier helpers.
- Fewest elements (Rule 4) / APP: `loyaltyDiscount` mass ≈ 15 is already minimal for a conditional discount; rewrites don't lower it and cost clarity (Rule 2 trumps APP).

Per the mandatory-attempt rule, I evaluated naming, all four Simple Design rules, and APP mass on the Green-touched code path; the genuinely tempting refactor is off-limits because it would alter behavior reserved for a pending test.

Confirming tests remain green (unchanged file):

content.text:
## Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring:** None applied this cycle — documented below.

**Reasoning:**
- The Green phase added no implementation (the `< 2 years` boundary test passed on the pre-existing `>= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` guard), so there is no new code to clean up.
- Naming on the touched path is already intent-revealing: `loyaltyDiscount`, `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` (2), `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE` (0.2).
- No duplication among modifier helpers (Rule 3).
- `loyaltyDiscount` APP mass ≈ 15 is minimal for a conditional discount; rewrites don't lower it and would cost clarity — Rule 2 trumps APP (Rule 4).
- The one real smell in the file, `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` (lines 86–97), is a self-documented TEMPORARY hack reserved for upcoming tests (todo line 249 "first-insurance surcharge on the policy base premium" and line 254 "curse surcharge only to the cursed item"). Refactoring it now would change behavior, violating the green-only discipline of Refactor. It must be driven out by its own test.

**Mass Change:** n/a (no code changed).

**Tests:** 19 passing, 26 todo — all green.

- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_07-22-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts
